sanskrit_devanagari:
प्रसादे सर्वदुःखानां हानिरस्योपजायते ।
प्रसन्नचेतसो ह्याशु बुद्धिः पर्यवतिष्ठते ॥ ६५ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
prasāde sarva-duḥkhānāṁ hānir asyopajāyate |
prasanna-cetaso hy āśu buddhiḥ paryavatiṣṭhate || 65 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape, body text byte-identical to canonical)
exact (text body identical; uses single danda | as terminator)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.65।।)
IAST exact (Devanagari rendered without transliteration marks; IAST body matches: 'prasade sarva-duhkhanam hanir asya upajayate prasanna-cetaso hy asu buddhih paryavatisthate')

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.065 = MBh_06,024.065). Body text identical: 'prasāde sarvaduḥkhānāṃ hānir asyopajāyate / prasannacetaso hy āśu buddhiḥ paryavatiṣṭhate'. GRETIL writes 'sarvaduḥkhānāṃ' / 'prasannacetaso' as compounds without hyphens where vedabase uses 'sarva-duḥkhānāṁ' / 'prasanna-cetaso'; same compounds.

anvaya.meaning: in serenity / when there is mental clarity
of sorrows / of miseries (in compound sarva-duḥkhānāṁ: 'of all sorrows')
of the mind (in compound prasanna-cetasaḥ: 'of one whose mind is serene')

translations.verbatim_quote: For one thus satisfied [in Kṛṣṇa consciousness], the threefold miseries of material existence exist no longer; in such satisfied consciousness, one's intelligence is soon well established.
By divine grace comes the peace in which all sorrows end, and the intellect of such a person of tranquil mind soon becomes firmly established in God.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Becoming satisfied means cessation of all material activities. For a person who is detached from such an external surface, the mind is calm; therefore, sorrow does not arise. Where there is no sorrow, the intelligence is fixed.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (short excerpt of translator-rendered Shankara bhashya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Prasade, when there is serenity; upajayate, there follows; hanih, eradication; asya sarva-duhkhanam, of all his, the sannyasin's, sorrow on the physical and other planes. Moreover, (this is so) hi, because; buddhih, the wisdom; prasanna-cetasah, of one who has a serene mind, of one whose mind is poised in the Self; asu, soon; pari-avatisthate, becomes firmly established; remains steady (avatisthate) totally (pari), like the sky, i.e. it becomes unmoving in its very nature as the Self.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as the consequence of the prasāda attained in 2.64. When serenity is present, all sorrows — physical, mental, and other — cease. The buddhi (discriminating wisdom) of the serene-minded soon becomes firmly established, like the unmoving sky. Shankara's gloss on paryavatiṣṭhate emphasises totality (pari = totally) and stillness (avatiṣṭhate = remains steady) — the buddhi does not merely function, it stops being moved by anything outside its own nature.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (short excerpt of translator-rendered Ramanuja bhashya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: When the mind of this person gets serene, he gets rid of all sorrows originating from contact with matter. For, in respect of the peson whose mind is serene, i.e., is free from the evil which is antagonistic to the vision of the self, the Buddhi, having the pure self for its object, becomes established immediately. Thus, when the mind is serene, the loss of all sorrow surely arises.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse as the chain that begins from the focus on the Supreme prescribed in 2.61. When the mind is serene (free from the saṁskāras antagonistic to the vision of the self), all sorrows arising from contact with matter cease, and the buddhi — taking the pure self as its object — becomes established immediately. The verse names the cessation of suffering and the stabilisation of buddhi as joint products of serenity, both following structurally from the same precondition.

positions.rendering: for one thus satisfied [in Kṛṣṇa consciousness] (the operator's state of devotional satisfaction)
by divine grace (the favour of God)
when there is serenity (the operator's mental state)
when the mind of this person gets serene (the operator's mental state, following from focus on the Supreme)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: prasāde is the locative of prasāda, continuing the term from 2.64. Shankara and Ramanuja both render it as the operator's serenity (the mental state). Mukundananda renders it as divine grace (the favour received from God), Prabhupada as devotional satisfaction (a hybrid of state and reception). The verse's lexical chain prasāde → prasanna-cetasaḥ in the same verse strongly favours the operator's-state reading, since prasanna-cetasaḥ unambiguously means 'serene-minded' as the operator's quality. Shankara's reading is therefore the lexically tightest. The grace-rendering is consistent with Vaishnava theology but stretches the Sanskrit further.

positions.rendering: becomes firmly established; remains steady totally, like the sky, unmoving in its very nature as the Self
becomes established immediately, having the pure self for its object

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: paryavatiṣṭhate (pari + ava + sthā) is a compound verb: 'totally + down + stand'. Shankara's gloss preserves the totality and the stillness explicitly ('remains steady totally, like the sky'). The buddhi does not merely function under serenity — it stops being moved at all by anything outside its own nature. Modern translations preserve the establishedness but lose the totality nuance. The Sanskrit's rhetorical force is the strong claim of complete stabilisation, not merely improved functioning.

literal_meaning: In serenity, the destruction of all sorrows arises for him. For the intelligence of the serene-minded swiftly stabilises completely.
traditional_meaning_consensus: This verse names the consequence of the prasāda attained in 2.64. Two structural results follow from the operator's serenity: (1) the cessation of all sorrows (sarva-duḥkhānāṁ hāniḥ), and (2) the swift, total stabilisation of buddhi (intelligence, discriminating faculty). Shankara's gloss on paryavatiṣṭhate emphasises the totality — buddhi does not merely improve, it stops being moved by anything outside its own nature, like the unmoving sky. Ramanuja reads the chain back to 2.61: when the mind has been focussed on the Supreme, the saṁskāras antagonistic to self-knowledge are burned out, the mind becomes serene, sorrows cease, and buddhi takes the pure self as its object. Gaudiya Vaishnava reading folds prasāda into devotional satisfaction. All traditions agree the structural claim: serenity produces both the cessation of suffering and stable judgment, swiftly and together. Frantic action does not produce stable judgment; settled action does.

remaining_gaps: Shankara's full Sanskrit bhashya not pulled — only translator-rendered (Gambhirananda) excerpt was machine-extractable.
